title: Two Friends Argue About Reading Fiction
summary: Two friends disagree about whether reading fiction is a waste of time, and the argument moves from defensive to surprisingly thoughtful.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: I saw on your feed that you've stopped reading novels entirely. Is that a phase, or is it actually a position now?

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: It's a position. I haven't read a novel in eighteen months, and I don't miss it. I'd rather spend that time on books that teach me something I didn't already know.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: That assumes novels don't teach you anything. I'd push back on that quite hard. Some of the most important things I've learned about how other people think came from fiction, not from a textbook.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: I'm not saying novels are worthless. I'm saying that for me, hour for hour, non-fiction gives me more. If I have a free Sunday afternoon, I'd rather understand how the global shipping system works than follow a made-up character around a made-up town.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Okay, but you're describing fiction as if it's just entertainment. Good fiction is the opposite of escapism. It makes you sit with situations and feelings you'd normally avoid. Non-fiction tells you about other people. Fiction puts you inside them.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: That's a nice line, but is there evidence for it? I keep hearing people claim novels build empathy, but I've not seen anything that proves it.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Actually, there's a fair amount of research that suggests literary fiction improves the ability to read other people's emotions. It's not a huge effect, but it's repeatable. I'd send you the studies, if you'd actually read them.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: Touché. Send them. But even if it's true, the effect must be small. Plenty of people who don't read fiction are good with other humans, and plenty of people who do read fiction are awful.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Sure. Reading fiction is one input, not a guaranteed transformation. But you've moved the goalposts. You started by saying novels don't teach anything. Now you're saying the effect is small. Those are different claims.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: Fair enough. I was overstating my position. What I really meant is that I've been disappointed by recent novels. I started three this year and didn't finish any of them. They all felt like the writer had something to prove rather than something to say.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Now we're getting somewhere. That's not 'fiction is useless' — that's 'I haven't picked the right novels'. The first one is a philosophical position; the second is a practical problem with an obvious solution.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: Possibly. What would you recommend, then?

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Honestly? Stop picking novels that are getting prizes this year. Read something at least twenty years old. If a book is still in print after twenty years, somebody other than the publisher's marketing team thought it was good.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: That's actually useful advice. The novels I gave up on were all recent prize-winners.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Try a short one first. Try one with a story you wouldn't normally pick. And don't expect every novel to change your life — most won't, just like most non-fiction books won't either.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: Okay. I'll try one. But if it's bad, I'm coming back to argue with you again.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Fair. And I'll admit something, since we're being honest. I read too much fiction and not enough non-fiction. I sometimes finish a novel and realise I haven't read anything practical in months. The best argument against my position is that I should probably take more of yours.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: So we're saying the same thing from opposite directions — we both need a better balance.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: That sounds suspiciously like agreement, which wasn't where I expected this conversation to go.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: Don't get used to it. Send me three novel recommendations by tomorrow, and I'll send you three non-fiction ones. We'll meet again in two months and see if either of us has changed.

body.speaker: Hana
body.text: Deal. And read at least one of them before you tell me they're all rubbish.

body.speaker: Niko
body.text: I'll read them. I will probably still argue with you, but I'll read them first.

annotations.meaningVi: đó có phải là một giai đoạn (tạm thời) không
annotations.exampleEn: Is that a phase, or is it a real position now?

annotations.meaningVi: tôi sẽ phản bác lại điều đó
annotations.exampleEn: I'd push back on that quite hard.

annotations.meaningVi: tính theo từng giờ, so sánh giờ với giờ
annotations.exampleEn: Hour for hour, non-fiction gives me more.
I don't want to follow a made-up character around a made-up town.

annotations.meaningVi: sự trốn thoát khỏi thực tại
annotations.exampleEn: Good fiction is the opposite of escapism.
Some research suggests novels help build empathy.
Literary fiction is different from genre fiction in tone and intent.

annotations.meaningVi: lặp lại được (kết quả nghiên cứu)
annotations.exampleEn: The effect is small but repeatable across studies.

annotations.meaningVi: trúng rồi (thừa nhận đối phương ghi điểm)
đổi luôn mục tiêu (giữa tranh luận)
annotations.exampleEn: You've moved the goalposts — those are different claims.

annotations.meaningVi: phóng đại quan điểm của tôi
annotations.exampleEn: I was overstating my position when I said all fiction is useless.

annotations.phrase: something to prove rather than something to say
annotations.meaningVi: có gì đó để chứng tỏ hơn là có gì đó để nói
annotations.pronunciation: /ˈsʌmθɪŋ tə pruːv ˈrɑːðə ðən ˈsʌmθɪŋ tə seɪ/
annotations.exampleEn: Those novels felt like the writer had something to prove rather than something to say.
That's a practical problem with an obvious solution.

annotations.meaningVi: đội tiếp thị của nhà xuất bản
annotations.exampleEn: If a book outlasts the publisher's marketing team, it's probably good.
Don't expect every novel to change your life.

annotations.meaningVi: có vẻ giống như đang đồng ý một cách đáng ngờ
annotations.exampleEn: That sounds suspiciously like agreement, which I didn't expect.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech and Hedging in Argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại cho thấy cách tường thuật và lập luận lịch sự: 'You started by saying novels don't teach anything. Now you're saying the effect is small. Those are different claims', 'What I really meant is that I've been disappointed', 'I keep hearing people claim novels build empathy'. Các cụm này (started by saying, what I really meant, I keep hearing) cho phép tóm tắt và phản biện một cách rõ ràng mà không trực tiếp tấn công.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue shows polite argument and reported speech: 'You started by saying novels don't teach anything. Now you're saying the effect is small.', 'What I really meant is that I've been disappointed', 'I keep hearing people claim novels build empathy'. These phrases (started by saying, what I really meant, I keep hearing) summarise and rebut without direct attack.

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional and 'would' for Hypothetical Preferences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người nói dùng 'would' và câu điều kiện loại 2 để bày tỏ ưu tiên giả định: 'I'd rather spend that time on books', 'I'd rather understand how the global shipping system works', 'If I have a free Sunday afternoon, I'd rather...'. Cấu trúc: 'would rather + verb' (hoặc 'if + present, would + verb'). Đây là cách lịch sự để bày tỏ sở thích.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Speakers use 'would' and second conditional for hypothetical preferences: 'I'd rather spend that time on books', 'I'd rather understand how the global shipping system works'. Form: 'would rather + verb'. This is a polite way to state preferences without forcing them on others.

translationVi:
Hana: Tôi thấy trên trang của bạn rằng bạn đã ngừng đọc tiểu thuyết hoàn toàn. Đó là một giai đoạn, hay thực sự là quan điểm bây giờ?

Niko: Là quan điểm. Tôi không đọc tiểu thuyết trong mười tám tháng, và tôi không nhớ nó. Tôi thà dùng thời gian đó cho những cuốn sách dạy tôi điều tôi chưa biết.

Hana: Điều đó giả định rằng tiểu thuyết không dạy bạn gì. Tôi sẽ phản bác lại khá mạnh. Một số điều quan trọng nhất tôi học được về cách người khác suy nghĩ đến từ tiểu thuyết, không phải từ sách giáo khoa.

Niko: Tôi không nói tiểu thuyết vô giá trị. Tôi nói rằng với tôi, tính theo từng giờ, sách phi hư cấu cho tôi nhiều hơn. Nếu có một buổi chiều Chủ nhật rảnh, tôi thà hiểu hệ thống vận tải toàn cầu hoạt động ra sao còn hơn theo dõi một nhân vật bịa quanh một thị trấn bịa.

Hana: Được, nhưng bạn đang mô tả tiểu thuyết như chỉ để giải trí. Tiểu thuyết hay là ngược lại với trốn thoát. Nó khiến bạn ngồi với những tình huống và cảm xúc bạn thường tránh. Sách phi hư cấu kể cho bạn về người khác. Tiểu thuyết đặt bạn vào trong họ.

Niko: Câu hay đấy, nhưng có bằng chứng không? Tôi cứ nghe người ta nói tiểu thuyết xây dựng đồng cảm, nhưng tôi chưa thấy gì chứng minh.

Hana: Thực ra có khá nhiều nghiên cứu cho thấy tiểu thuyết văn học cải thiện khả năng đọc cảm xúc người khác. Hiệu ứng không lớn, nhưng lặp lại được. Tôi sẽ gửi bạn các nghiên cứu, nếu bạn thực sự đọc.

Niko: Trúng rồi. Gửi đi. Nhưng dù có đúng, hiệu ứng chắc nhỏ. Nhiều người không đọc tiểu thuyết vẫn giỏi với con người, và nhiều người đọc tiểu thuyết lại tệ.

Hana: Đồng ý. Đọc tiểu thuyết là một đầu vào, không phải sự biến đổi đảm bảo. Nhưng bạn đã chuyển mục tiêu rồi. Lúc đầu bạn nói tiểu thuyết không dạy gì. Giờ bạn nói hiệu ứng nhỏ. Đó là hai luận điểm khác nhau.

Niko: Đúng. Tôi đang phóng đại quan điểm. Điều tôi thực sự muốn nói là tôi đã thất vọng với các tiểu thuyết gần đây. Tôi bắt đầu ba cuốn năm nay và không hoàn thành cuốn nào. Tất cả đều cảm giác như tác giả có điều cần chứng tỏ hơn là điều cần nói.

Hana: Giờ chúng ta đang đi tới đâu đó. Đó không phải 'tiểu thuyết vô dụng' — đó là 'tôi chưa chọn đúng tiểu thuyết'. Cái đầu là quan điểm triết học; cái sau là vấn đề thực tế với giải pháp hiển nhiên.

Niko: Có thể. Vậy bạn khuyến nghị gì?

Hana: Thật lòng à? Ngừng chọn tiểu thuyết đang được trao giải năm nay. Đọc gì đó ít nhất hai mươi năm tuổi. Nếu một cuốn sách vẫn còn được in sau hai mươi năm, ai đó ngoài đội tiếp thị của nhà xuất bản đã nghĩ nó hay.

Niko: Lời khuyên hữu ích đấy. Các tiểu thuyết tôi bỏ đều là sách thắng giải gần đây.

Hana: Thử một cuốn ngắn trước. Thử một cuốn với câu chuyện bạn thường không chọn. Và đừng kỳ vọng mọi tiểu thuyết sẽ thay đổi cuộc đời — đa số không, cũng như đa số sách phi hư cấu cũng không.

Niko: Được. Tôi sẽ thử một cuốn. Nhưng nếu dở, tôi sẽ quay lại cãi với bạn.

Hana: Công bằng. Và tôi sẽ thừa nhận một điều, vì chúng ta đang thẳng thắn. Tôi đọc quá nhiều tiểu thuyết và không đủ phi hư cấu. Đôi khi tôi đọc xong một cuốn tiểu thuyết và nhận ra tôi đã không đọc gì thực tế trong nhiều tháng. Luận điểm hay nhất chống lại quan điểm của tôi là tôi nên có thêm quan điểm của bạn.

Niko: Vậy chúng ta đang nói cùng một điều từ hai hướng đối lập — cả hai cần cân bằng tốt hơn.

Hana: Nghe giống đồng ý một cách đáng ngờ, không phải nơi tôi nghĩ cuộc trò chuyện này sẽ đến.

Niko: Đừng quen. Gửi tôi ba gợi ý tiểu thuyết trước ngày mai, tôi sẽ gửi bạn ba cuốn phi hư cấu. Hai tháng nữa gặp lại xem ai trong chúng ta đã thay đổi.

Hana: Thoả thuận. Và đọc ít nhất một cuốn trước khi bảo tôi tất cả là rác rưởi.

Niko: Tôi sẽ đọc. Tôi có thể vẫn cãi với bạn, nhưng tôi sẽ đọc trước.

questions.prompt: How long has Niko stopped reading novels?
questions.explanation: Niko says 'I haven't read a novel in eighteen months.'

questions.prompt: Why has Niko stopped reading novels?
options: He prefers books that teach him something he didn't already know
questions.explanation: Niko says 'I'd rather spend that time on books that teach me something I didn't already know.'
questions.hint: Niko's reason is about learning value.

questions.prompt: What is Hana's main defence of fiction?
options: Fiction is more entertaining than non-fiction
Non-fiction tells you about other people; fiction puts you inside them
Fiction makes you a better writer
questions.explanation: Hana says 'Non-fiction tells you about other people. Fiction puts you inside them.'
questions.hint: Her line is a precise contrast.

questions.prompt: What does Niko initially demand of Hana's empathy claim?
options: Evidence that fiction actually builds empathy
questions.explanation: Niko says 'is there evidence for it? I keep hearing people claim novels build empathy, but I've not seen anything that proves it.'
questions.hint: Niko is sceptical and asks for proof.

questions.prompt: What does Hana say about research on literary fiction and empathy?
options: The effect is large and certain
There is a fair amount of research suggesting it improves emotional reading; the effect is small but repeatable
questions.explanation: Hana says 'there's a fair amount of research that suggests literary fiction improves the ability to read other people's emotions. It's not a huge effect, but it's repeatable.'
questions.hint: Hana describes the effect honestly — small but real.

questions.prompt: What does Niko admit about his initial position?
options: He was overstating his position — he really meant he'd been disappointed by recent novels
questions.explanation: Niko says 'I was overstating my position. What I really meant is that I've been disappointed by recent novels.'
questions.hint: Niko admits a softer real position.

questions.prompt: How does Hana characterise the difference between Niko's two claims?
options: The first is a philosophical position; the second is a practical problem with an obvious solution
questions.explanation: Hana says 'The first one is a philosophical position; the second is a practical problem with an obvious solution.'

questions.prompt: What is Hana's main recommendation for choosing novels?
options: Read the prize-winners of the current year
Read something at least twenty years old that is still in print
Read whatever everyone else is reading
questions.explanation: Hana says 'Stop picking novels that are getting prizes this year. Read something at least twenty years old.'
questions.hint: Her rule is about age and survival.

questions.prompt: What were all the novels Niko gave up on?
questions.explanation: Niko says 'The novels I gave up on were all recent prize-winners.'

questions.prompt: What does Hana admit about her own reading habits?
options: She reads less than she should
She reads too much fiction and not enough non-fiction
She has never finished a non-fiction book
questions.explanation: Hana says 'I read too much fiction and not enough non-fiction.'
questions.hint: Her admission mirrors Niko's complaint in reverse.

questions.prompt: What deal do they make at the end?
options: They will swap recommendations — Niko three novels, Hana three non-fiction — and meet again in two months
They will write a joint review
They will never discuss reading again
questions.explanation: Niko says 'Send me three novel recommendations by tomorrow, and I'll send you three non-fiction ones. We'll meet again in two months.'
questions.hint: An exchange and a future meeting are agreed.

questions.prompt: How does the argument actually evolve?
options: Both speakers shift toward each other and admit weaknesses in their original positions
Niko refuses to change at all
questions.explanation: Niko admits overstating; Hana admits she reads too much fiction. Both move toward 'better balance.'
questions.hint: Look at the admissions both make in the second half.

questions.prompt: What does the dialogue suggest about good disagreement?
options: Stick rigidly to your first position no matter what
Use evidence, ask for specifics, and adjust your claim when the other person points out a contradiction
Always agree to keep the peace
questions.explanation: The dialogue shows Hana asking for evidence Niko's claim that fiction doesn't teach, then Niko softening; Niko pointing out 'small effect' and Hana noting he's moved the goalposts.
questions.hint: Look at the techniques both speakers use — citing studies, identifying contradictions, conceding ground.

cloze.template:
Hana: I saw that you've stopped reading novels entirely. Is that a {{b1}}, or is it actually a position now?
Niko: It's a position. I'd rather spend that time on books that teach me something I didn't already know.
Hana: That assumes novels don't teach you anything. I'd {{b2}} back on that quite hard.
Niko: Hour for hour, non-fiction gives me more.
Hana: Good fiction is the opposite of {{b3}}. Non-fiction tells you about other people. Fiction puts you inside them.
Niko: Is there {{b4}} for it? I keep hearing people claim novels build empathy, but I've not seen anything that proves it.
Hana: There's research that suggests literary fiction improves the ability to read other people's emotions. It's not a huge effect, but it's {{b5}}.
Niko: Plenty of people who don't read fiction are good with other humans.
Hana: Sure. But you've moved the {{b6}}. You started by saying novels don't teach anything. Now you're saying the effect is small. Those are different claims.
Niko: Fair enough. I was {{b7}} my position. What I really meant is that I've been disappointed by recent novels. They felt like the writer had something to prove rather than something to {{b8}}.
Hana: That's not 'fiction is useless' — that's 'I haven't picked the right novels'. Read something at least twenty years old. If a book is still in {{b9}} after twenty years, somebody other than the publisher's marketing team thought it was good.
Niko: Useful advice.
Hana: Don't expect every novel to change your {{b10}} — most won't, just like most non-fiction books won't either.

blanks.explanation: 'A phase' specifically means a temporary stage — fits the question whether the change is permanent.
'Push back' is the fixed phrasal verb for politely disagreeing or resisting.
'Escapism' is the exact word Hana uses to contrast with serious fiction.
'Is there evidence for it' is Niko's exact phrase — a request for empirical support.
'Repeatable' is the scientific term — meaning the result is reproducible across studies.
'Moved the goalposts' is the fixed idiom — to change the criteria mid-argument.
'Overstating' is the exact word Niko uses — making a claim stronger than warranted.
'Something to prove rather than something to say' is the exact contrast Niko makes — the second half is 'say'.
'In print' is the standard phrase for a book still being published — Hana's exact word.
'Change your life' is the fixed cliché that Hana is deliberately deflating.

criticalThinkingQuestion: By the end, both Hana and Niko admit they need more of what the other reads. Is reading a balanced mix of fiction and non-fiction better than going deep on one — or is depth in a single genre worth more than the imagined balance?
